picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: GOOGL is the single highest-scoring name in the momentum screen (+27.24% YTD vs SPY +7.09%) with a blowout Q1 print — EPS of $5.11 vs $2.63 consensus — driven by Google Cloud accelerating to +63% YoY growth. Both Druckenmiller (added 276.7%) and Ackman held it as a core position, validating the AI monetization thesis. At ~$385–389 today, it is consolidating just below its 52-week high of $402 post-CPI selloff, offering a technically clean add point.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Cloud growth trajectory and Search AI monetization update; also Google I/O product announcements in May
picks.catalystWindow: Next 4–10 weeks
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~22–23x NTM EPS, which is below its 5-year average of ~25x; post-CPI dip to $383–389 zone is an attractive add window given the fundamental acceleration in Cloud
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud revenue growth decelerates below 40% YoY, or DOJ antitrust ruling forces structural search remedy that impairs default distribution economics
picks.keyRisk: DOJ antitrust remedies — structural separation of Chrome/Android or default search agreements — could impair the core monetization engine

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: AVGO is the cleanest custom-silicon AI play in the universe — AI semiconductor revenue surged 106% YoY to $8.4B in Q1, with Q2 guided to $22B total (+47% YoY) and CEO Hock Tan projecting $100B+ in custom AI chip revenue by 2027. Trading at ~$417 today (52-week range $215–$437), Citi just raised its PT to $500. FCF of $8B/quarter and a fresh $10B buyback program provide capital return support. The OpenAI $18B financing snag is a known risk but does not threaten Broadcom's diversified hyperscaler base (Google, Meta, Apple custom chips).
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2026 earnings (June 5) — confirmation of $22B guide and AI chip trajectory toward $10.7B
picks.catalystWindow: Next 3–4 weeks
picks.entryNote: At ~$417, AVGO is 4.6% below its 52-week high of $437 and consolidating above its 50dma; forward P/E ~30–32x on FY2027 estimates is reasonable for a 40%+ revenue growth profile with 75%+ gross margins
picks.exitTrigger: AI chip revenue guide deceleration below 30% YoY, or hyperscaler capex freeze announcement that cuts AVGO's ASIC design pipeline
picks.keyRisk: OpenAI financing deal collapse (~$18B snag) and concentration risk — three hyperscaler customers represent majority of AI chip revenue

picks.name: Eli Lilly and Company
picks.rationale: LLY is a top-2 ranked name from the momentum screen — Q1 2026 EPS of $8.55 beat $6.85 consensus by 25%, with revenue up 56% YoY to $19.8B led by Mounjaro/Zepbound volume growth. Full-year guidance raised to $82–85B revenue and $35.50–$37.00 non-GAAP EPS. Non-GAAP gross margin of 82.6% is elite quality. In a late-cycle stagflation regime, GLP-1 demand is remarkably inelastic — patients do not stop GLP-1 treatment during recessions — making LLY a secular compounder with genuine defensive characteristics. The hot April CPI print has zero bearing on Lilly's fundamental outlook.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July/August) and potential Zepbound label expansion for cardiovascular outcomes; manufacturing capacity milestone updates
picks.entryNote: Forward P/E ~32–35x justified by 41% consensus EPS growth in 2026 and multi-year CAGR visibility; LLY has pulled back from its prior highs and is trading at a reasonable entry relative to estimate revision momentum
picks.exitTrigger: Medicare IRA GLP-1 price negotiation triggers >20% net price cut, or a significant manufacturing execution failure that delays supply ramp
picks.keyRisk: CMS Medicare drug price negotiation — GLP-1s could be selected for the next negotiation cycle, compressing net realized pricing

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: XOM is the highest-conviction Energy expression in this macro regime — Iran war escalation has pushed WTI to $97–107/barrel range with Brent at $111+, and today's hot CPI print directly validates the energy price pass-through thesis. XLE gained 37% in Q1 2026 and Energy is now upgraded to Tier 1 in the sector rotation framework. Berkshire added CVX (same thesis) while the Wikipedia energy crisis data confirms US crude exports surged to nearly 12.9 million barrels/day. XOM's integrated model (upstream + refining + chemical) captures margin at every point in the chain. Strong dividend (~3.5%) and buyback program provide downside support.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with $97–107 WTI realized pricing expected to drive record upstream FCF; potential Iran escalation re-rating if combat operations resume
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing / next 6–8 weeks
picks.entryNote: XOM at ~11–12x forward earnings vs 10-year median of ~13–14x — modest discount to history despite a structurally better balance sheet post-Pioneer acquisition; Brent floor expected at $80–90/barrel even in ceasefire scenario
picks.exitTrigger: Iran ceasefire fully implemented and Strait of Hormuz reopens with OPEC+ simultaneously flooding supply, pushing Brent below $70; or structural demand destruction from global recession
picks.keyRisk: Diplomatic resolution to Iran conflict (ceasefire + Strait reopening) causing rapid crude price collapse toward $70–75

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is the highest-quality defense prime in the universe — record order backlogs confirmed at Q1 earnings per BBC/IranIntl reporting, and the second Trump administration has reportedly quadrupled weapons production targets to address defensive system exhaust from Iran war operations. RTX's Raytheon segment (Patriot missiles, interceptors, Stingers) is directly consuming government budgets, while Pratt & Whitney's aftermarket engine services provide a high-margin recurring revenue stream. The Iran war creates a multi-year replenishment cycle that outlasts the conflict itself regardless of ceasefire timing.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — order backlog update, Patriot/interceptor deliveries and contract awards from DoD supplemental budget
picks.catalystWindow: Next 6–10 weeks and ongoing through 2027
picks.entryNote: RTX trades at ~19–21x forward earnings, reasonable for an aerospace/defense prime with 8–10% revenue CAGR visibility; the defense sub-sector adds geopolitical optionality at a valuation far cheaper than software peers
picks.exitTrigger: Comprehensive Middle East peace agreement collapses defense supplemental budgets; or Pratt & Whitney GTF engine inspection liability expands materially beyond reserved amounts
picks.keyRisk: GTF engine powder metal inspection scope creep — if additional engine inspections are required, the liability could expand by $1–2B above current reserves

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: GS is the highest-beta expression of the Financials Tier 1 call — the Iran war has driven historic trading revenues (JPM's trading arm posted record $11.6B in Q1 revenue; across six largest US banks profits reached $47.7B in Q1 2026), and Goldman's FICC and equity trading desks are the most direct beneficiaries of elevated commodity/rate/geopolitical volatility. The hot April CPI print directly supports Goldman's NIM expansion and trading revenue through sustained rate volatility. Druckenmiller's massive new XLF position (5.5M shares, $300M) validates the financial sector thesis, and GS is the premier execution vehicle within that bet.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) — FICC trading revenue in a volatile rate/commodity/FX environment; investment banking pipeline recovery
picks.catalystWindow: Next 8–10 weeks
picks.entryNote: GS trades at ~1.3–1.4x book value — modest premium to historical median of 1.1–1.2x but justified by ROTE improvement and deregulatory tailwinds; forward P/E ~12–13x is below sector average
picks.exitTrigger: Market volatility collapses (VIX back to sub-15 sustained), crushing FICC trading revenues; or a credit event in private credit exposes balance sheet risk
picks.keyRisk: Abrupt end to geopolitical volatility and rate uncertainty that collapses trading revenues in H2 2026

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: AMZN is +17.48% YTD with AWS accelerating to +28% growth (fastest in 15 quarters) and a massive EPS beat of $2.78 vs $1.73 expected in Q1. The monetization question has been answered — AWS is the clearest AI infrastructure beneficiary outside semis, and retail margin recovery continues. AMZN is differentiated from GOOGL/AVGO in that it is primarily a cloud + logistics compounder rather than an ad-tech or semiconductor play, providing genuine sector diversification within the AI theme. It belongs in the portfolio as the AWS-infrastructure expression complementary to GOOGL Cloud and AVGO custom silicon.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July/August) — AWS growth rate confirmation above 28% and Prime/advertising margin expansion
picks.catalystWindow: Next 10–12 weeks
picks.entryNote: AMZN trades at ~35–38x NTM earnings — historically fair-to-moderate for the AWS+retail mix; the stock is consolidating off its YTD highs following CPI-driven tech selloff, providing a reasonable re-entry
picks.exitTrigger: AWS revenue growth decelerates below 20% YoY in two consecutive quarters, signaling hyperscaler share loss to Azure or GCP
picks.keyRisk: Tariff pass-through on retail COGS compresses North America retail margins while simultaneously slowing consumer discretionary spending

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is a best-in-class quality compounder that directly benefits from elevated rates, credit market activity, and the stagflation regime — its ratings, data analytics, and indices businesses all generate revenue proportional to market complexity and bond issuance volumes. In a 'higher for longer' environment where IG OAS stays tight (90–100bps) and corporate issuance remains robust, SPGI's ratings revenue is durable. The company's 70%+ operating margins and near-monopoly in credit ratings (with Moody's) create a structural moat that rates volatility amplifies rather than erodes. A Warsh-era Fed signaling more rate volatility directly benefits SPGI's analytics franchise.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — bond issuance volumes, Market Intelligence subscription growth, and Commodity Insights pricing power
picks.catalystWindow: Next 10–12 weeks
picks.entryNote: SPGI at ~29–31x forward earnings is modestly rich but warranted by its regulatory moat and 15%+ EPS CAGR; the stock has been a steady accumulator in the book since May 6 and fundamental thesis has only strengthened with hotter CPI
picks.exitTrigger: Credit market seizure that causes IG/HY issuance to collapse (recession scenario), materially cutting ratings revenue; or regulatory action breaking up the SPGI-Moody's duopoly
picks.keyRisk: Severe credit crunch or recession triggering a bond issuance freeze that cuts ratings and transaction-linked revenues by 20%+

picks.name: Vistra Corp.
picks.rationale: VST is the highest-conviction power generation play in the AI electricity demand supercycle — as a merchant power generator (not a regulated utility), Vistra captures upside from rising power prices rather than being rate-base constrained. Its nuclear fleet (through Energy Harbor acquisition) provides carbon-free baseload power exactly what hyperscalers are contracting for on long-term PPAs. In a regime where CPI is re-accelerating partly through energy services, Vistra passes through higher power prices directly to its bottom line, unlike regulated utilities capped by rate cases. This is a differentiated expression of the AI power thesis vs NEE in the defensive sleeve.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (early August) — power price realization in PJM and ERCOT markets; new hyperscaler PPA announcement
picks.catalystWindow: Next 2–3 months
picks.entryNote: VST trades at ~16–18x forward earnings — a meaningful discount to pure-play nuclear peers like CEG (~25x) — reflecting its merchant risk; that discount is the opportunity given locked-in PPA contracts and nuclear tax credit tailwinds
picks.exitTrigger: Power prices in PJM/ERCOT collapse due to renewable oversupply or demand miss; or nuclear plant unplanned outages materially above historical base rates
picks.keyRisk: Merchant power price exposure — a warm winter or recession-driven demand destruction could sharply compress realized power prices without regulated rate-base protection

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: META is the rare mega-cap where fundamentals lead price — Q1 2026 revenue was $56.31B (+33% YoY), net income $26.77B (+61%), and operating margin held at a pristine 41% despite AI buildout capex. At -6.48% YTD it is the only Mag-7 with negative price momentum despite accelerating earnings, making it the strongest catch-up candidate in the book. Advertising revenue is cyclically tied to nominal GDP — and in a stagflation regime, nominal GDP remains elevated even as real growth softens, supporting ad spending durability. ARK bought META in Q4 2025 further supporting the thesis from a revisions standpoint.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Q2 advertising revenue growth confirmation and Llama/AI Assistants monetization update
picks.catalystWindow: Next 10–12 weeks
picks.entryNote: META at ~19–21x NTM earnings — its cheapest multiple relative to earnings growth (PEG ~0.7x) of any mega-cap; the -6.48% YTD underperformance vs fundamentals creates a mean-reversion setup
picks.exitTrigger: Operating margin drops below 35% due to AI capex acceleration without clear monetization path, or EU digital markets act imposes structural data restrictions impairing targeting
picks.keyRisk: AI capex continues to accelerate beyond guidance while ad revenue growth decelerates, compressing the operating margin that is the bull case's cornerstone

picks.name: SPDR Gold Trust
picks.rationale: GLD is the single best hedge in this portfolio against the three dominant tail risks: Iran escalation, USD confidence crisis, and stagflation. Gold carries no credit risk, no earnings risk, and no duration risk — it is the perfect complement to a book heavy in AI growth names. With the April CPI print at 3.8% YoY decisively above consensus, real rates are being compressed even as nominal rates hold, a historically bullish combination for gold. Druckenmiller's USD bear thesis and Berkshire's record cash hoard both imply the same macro skepticism that GLD monetizes.
picks.catalyst: Iran escalation re-entry (Trump considering restarting combat operations per CNN); USD weakness persistence; Warsh FOMC tone on May 15 (hawkish = rate hike fear = gold positive via stagflation premium)
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing / next 2–4 weeks
picks.entryNote: Gold at multi-year highs but with real rates compressed and fiscal deficits expanding — historically this combination sustains gold's bid; not chasing a speculative move but holding structural allocation
picks.exitTrigger: Iran ceasefire fully resolves, USD DXY recovers sustainably above 106, and core CPI prints below 2.5% for two consecutive months
picks.keyRisk: Swift Iran diplomatic resolution + strong USD recovery compresses the geopolitical risk premium and real-rate support simultaneously

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD is the portfolio's quality income anchor — its factor exposure (value, profitability, dividend growth, low vol) is structurally negatively correlated to the AI momentum trade that dominates the growth book. In a late-cycle stagflation regime where growth multiples face compression, SCHD's blend of financials, industrials, healthcare, and consumer staples dividend growers provides a cushion. Yield ~3.5% is competitive with short-duration IG credit without the interest rate duration risk of TLT. SCHD has been a consistent carry-over in the book since May 7.
picks.catalyst: Sustained 'higher for longer' rate environment that rewards dividend growth stocks with pricing power over pure rate-sensitive yield names; Q2 dividend growth confirmations from core holdings
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing
picks.entryNote: SCHD at a historically reasonable valuation vs the broader market — its underlying holdings trade at ~14–15x forward earnings, a meaningful discount to the S&P 500's 21x; dividend yield of ~3.5% acts as a return floor
picks.exitTrigger: Aggressive Fed rate cuts below 2.5% that render dividend yields uncompetitive vs Treasuries while simultaneously lifting risk appetite into unprofitable growth names
picks.keyRisk: A dramatic shift to risk-on (Fed pivots dovish) could cause SCHD to underperform the growth book significantly, creating opportunity cost drag

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B
picks.rationale: BRK.B is the portfolio's Swiss Army knife defensive — its record ~$380B cash hoard (as of Q1 2026 earnings on May 4) generates risk-free yield and provides Greg Abel's team the capacity to acquire high-quality assets in any drawdown. The OxyChem acquisition ($9.5B closed Jan 2026) adds commodity/chemical real-asset exposure that benefits from the Iran energy shock. BRK.B is one of the few mega-caps trading below 1.5x book value with no meaningful AI multiple risk. In a risk-off tape, it historically outperforms the S&P 500 by 500–800bps on a drawdown basis, making it a genuine portfolio hedge.
picks.catalyst: Q1 2026 13F filing (May 15) — first full Abel-era disclosure of Q1 portfolio moves; any Abel commentary on capital deployment plans
picks.catalystWindow: Next 3 days (May 15 13F filing)
picks.entryNote: BRK.B at ~1.4–1.5x book value — historically below Buffett's self-imposed buyback threshold of 1.5x, suggesting downside is protected by the company's own buyback program; record cash earns ~5% risk-free, supporting intrinsic value accretion
picks.exitTrigger: Greg Abel makes a large, low-quality acquisition that destroys the balance sheet fortress; or Buffett resigns as Chairman creating an overhang on the conglomerate structure
picks.keyRisk: Large 'elephant-sized' acquisition at a premium multiple in a high-rate environment that deploys the cash hoard into a poor risk/reward opportunity

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: IEF provides the portfolio's sole fixed income / long-duration hedge — not as a rate-cut bet but as a tail-risk hedge against a sudden growth shock or credit event that forces a flight-to-safety bid into Treasuries. In the scenario where AI capex deflates or a credit market seizure triggers recession fears, IEF would rally sharply (duration ~7–8 years) even in a generally inflationary environment. The position is sized small (4%) precisely because the base case argues against duration — it is an asymmetric tail insurance position, not a macro call. Maintained from May 7–11 positioning at reduced size.
picks.catalyst: Recessionary growth shock (Q2 GDP surprise miss below 0%), credit spread widening event, or a risk-off flight from equities that forces institutional reallocation into Treasuries
picks.catalystWindow: Contingent / next 6–12 months
picks.entryNote: 10-year yield at 4.39% — IEF held at a historically attractive all-in yield for a defensive sleeve position; trimmed from prior allocation given hot CPI reducing probability of imminent rate cuts
picks.exitTrigger: 10-year yield breaks above 5.0% sustainably on renewed inflation acceleration, or Warsh explicitly signals rate hikes at May 15 assumption speech
picks.keyRisk: Hot CPI cycle continues and 30% rate hike probability rises further, driving the 10-year toward 5.0% and producing IEF mark-to-market losses

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: KO is the portfolio's inflation-resistant consumer staples anchor — a Berkshire top-5 holding (10.2% of disclosed portfolio) with proven ability to raise prices above CPI through global brand pricing power. In a stagflation regime where middle/lower income consumers are squeezed, KO's affordable beverages retain volume while pricing moves up, a classic stagflation winner. KO's 3%+ dividend yield is durable across economic cycles with 60+ consecutive years of dividend growth. As the most direct expression of the 'consumer staples defensive' exposure required in the defensive sleeve, KO rounds out the book with low correlation to the AI growth names.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — organic revenue growth confirmation above 6% and pricing power maintenance in a 3.8% CPI environment; dividend increase announcement
picks.catalystWindow: Next 10–12 weeks
picks.entryNote: KO trades at ~22–23x forward earnings vs its 10-year median of ~22x — fairly valued for a quality compounder; entry here is valuation-neutral, justified entirely by the defensive sleeve requirement and Berkshire validation
picks.exitTrigger: Organic volume growth turns negative for two consecutive quarters as consumers trade down from branded beverages; or obesity drug adoption materially reduces beverage consumption trends
picks.keyRisk: GLP-1 obesity drug adoption structurally reduces beverage/sugar consumption at scale, impairing KO's long-run volume growth across the portfolio

summary: The macro regime is late-cycle stagflation with an Iran war energy shock overlay — April CPI printed 3.8% YoY (hottest since May 2023), core CPI hit 0.4% MoM, rate hike odds are now at 30% per CME, and WTI crude swung between $88–$107 last week with Trump reportedly considering restarting combat operations. The 10-name growth book expresses this via three structural AI infrastructure names (GOOGL, AVGO, AMZN) that are differentiated across the value chain (cloud+search, custom silicon, hyperscaler cloud), two direct Iran-regime beneficiaries (XOM in Energy Tier 1, RTX in Defense/Industrials), two financials that benefit from rate volatility and trading revenue (GS, SPGI), one healthcare secular compounder (LLY), one AI power merchant (VST), and one catch-up AI/ad-tech play (META). Core carryovers from the prior week — GOOGL, AVGO, LLY, GS, AMZN, RTX, XOM, VST, SPGI — all retain intact theses reinforced by today's CPI; META is elevated from a medium-conviction hold to a ranked growth position given its fundamental/price momentum divergence creating the most attractive risk/reward in Communication Services. The defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) spans gold, dividend equity, a cash-fortress conglomerate, intermediate Treasuries as tail-risk insurance, and a consumer staples quality compounder — the dominant risk being underwritten is that a Warsh hawkish pivot or Iran re-escalation reprices growth multiples and forces a defensive rotation the sleeve is designed to partially cushion.
diversificationNote: The growth book spans 6 distinct GICS sectors: Communication Services (GOOGL, META — two names sharing the ad/AI theme but with different revenue models), Information Technology (AVGO), Health Care (LLY), Energy (XOM), Industrials (RTX), Financials (GS, SPGI — two names accepted given the sector's Tier 1 ranking and their differentiated catalysts: trading revenue vs. ratings/data analytics), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN), and Utilities (VST); GOOGL and AVGO share AI infrastructure secular exposure but operate at entirely different points in the value chain (cloud software monetization vs. custom ASIC silicon). The defensive sleeve spans five distinct exposures — gold (GLD), broad dividend/quality equity (SCHD), conglomerate/cash fortress (BRK.B), intermediate Treasuries as tail-risk insurance (IEF), and consumer staples quality compounder (KO) — with no duplicate exposure.

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Alphabet delivered Q1 2026 EPS of $5.11 — a +93.6% beat vs. consensus — on 21.8% revenue growth, with Google Cloud surging 63% to $20B and Cloud backlog nearly doubling to $462B. The AI monetization flywheel (Search AI Overviews, Gemini embedded in Workspace, Cloud AI workloads) is compounding into the world's deepest distribution network while forward estimates have been aggressively revised upward. Ackman, Viking Global, Tiger Global, and Coatue all hold GOOGL as a core position, providing heavyweight institutional validation.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with continued Cloud re-acceleration and AI Overviews monetization data; April CPI print May 12 as a benign macro relief catalyst
picks.catalystWindow: Next 4 weeks (May 12 CPI) + next 2 quarters (Q2 earnings)
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$337, up ~30% over 6 months vs. SPY; forward P/E of approximately 20x for a business growing cloud at 63% is deeply undemanding relative to peers — not technically extended given the Q1 earnings reset higher
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 30% YoY for two consecutive quarters, or regulatory breakup order targeting Search materially advances in courts
picks.keyRisk: AI-driven search disruption (Perplexity, OpenAI SearchGPT) eroding query volume and CPCs faster than Cloud offsets

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom's Q1 FY2026 beat of +9% vs. estimates on 29% revenue growth (semiconductor segment +52%) reflects structurally durable demand for custom AI ASICs from Google, Meta, and ByteDance — the ASIC TAM is the most defensible moat in semiconductors. VMware's infrastructure software suite layered onto the chip business produces high-margin recurring revenue that insulates earnings in a cyclical downturn. 48 of 50 analysts rate it a Buy. Today's dip on OpenAI chip-deal financing noise ($418 vs. 52-week high $437) is a tactical entry opportunity rather than a thesis break, as the OpenAI relationship is a marginal contributor — the hyperscaler ASIC engine (Google TPU, Meta MTIA) is the core.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2026 earnings (est. June 5, 2026) — next formal update on AI ASIC backlog and VMware integration margins
picks.catalystWindow: Next 4 weeks for post-noise stabilization; June 5 earnings event
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$418 on May 8, pulled ~4% intraday on OpenAI deal financing headlines — 52-week range $203–$437; forward P/E ~43x on 25%+ growth is premium but justified given VMware recurring revenue contribution; entry here is meaningfully below ATH
picks.exitTrigger: Loss of a second major hyperscaler ASIC design win (beyond OpenAI noise) or VMware customer churn accelerating above 10% annually
picks.keyRisk: OpenAI custom chip financing difficulties signal a broader risk that non-hyperscaler ASIC customers lack balance sheet depth to fund $10B+ chip programs, reducing future ASIC TAM

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: Meta's Q1 2026 revenue grew 33% YoY with record operating margins as AI-driven ad ranking tools (Andromeda, Advantage+) continue extracting higher CPMs from the same inventory base — a monetization flywheel requiring zero incremental distribution cost. Management's capex raise post-Q1 signals confidence in monetization ROI rather than speculative spending. Bill Ackman initiated META as an 11.4% portfolio position in Q4 2025 — his largest new buy in years — citing the AI-ad flywheel and dominant platform moat.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with AI ad monetization metrics and Reality Labs cost discipline; ongoing AI advertising tool rollout to SMBs via Meta Advantage+
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing (AI ad product cycle) + Q2 earnings late July
picks.entryNote: Gross margins above 80%, FCF generation elite; forward P/E approximately 25x for 30%+ revenue growth implies a PEG ratio below 1.0 — manageable multiple for the quality of compounding; technically consolidating above prior resistance
picks.exitTrigger: Two consecutive quarters of ad revenue growth decelerating below 15%, or regulatory break-up of WhatsApp/Instagram from Facebook core
picks.keyRisk: Generative AI-native social platforms (character.ai, TikTok successors) capture Gen-Z engagement at scale, structurally reducing Meta's time-on-app metrics

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman Sachs is the highest-beta expression of the macro regime's most favorable impulses: a steepening yield curve (+50bp 10Y/2Y spread), HY OAS near historically tight 277bp supporting active M&A and IPO deal pipelines, and capital markets revenue surging as the Iran War creates advisory demand in energy, defense, and geopolitical M&A. The Financials sector (XLF) received Druckenmiller's single largest buy in Q4 2025, and Lone Pine Capital holds Financials as its largest sector allocation. Record blended S&P margins at 13.4% also support GS's asset/wealth management fee revenue.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) with investment banking revenue disclosures — IPO/M&A advisory backlog is the key metric given elevated deal activity
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing (deal market open) + Q2 earnings mid-July
picks.entryNote: Financials sector trades at a discount to the index per the macro research; GS at roughly 13x forward earnings vs. historical 11-14x range is fair-to-attractive for a capital markets cycle leader; has been a consistent top-5 hold since May 4
picks.exitTrigger: HY OAS widens above 450bp (stress signal), 10Y/2Y yield curve inverts again, or Q2 investment banking revenue declines more than 20% QoQ
picks.keyRisk: Iran War escalation triggers a risk-off credit event that freezes deal markets and widens spreads sharply — GS is the most direct casualty in that scenario

picks.name: Eli Lilly and Company
picks.rationale: Eli Lilly's tirzepatide franchise (Mounjaro/Zepbound) is structurally independent of the AI capex cycle and Iran War inflation shock — demand for GLP-1 weight-loss and diabetes therapy vastly exceeds supply through at least 2027. The regulatory moat (clinical data superiority, manufacturing scale barriers, early-2030s patent protection) makes this a multi-decade compounder. Healthcare sector forward P/E of ~16x vs. S&P at 20.9x offers valuation support, and LLY has de-rated from 2024 peak levels providing a better entry — confirmed by the risk officer's designation as a top defensive opportunity.
picks.catalyst: Phase 3 readout for orforglipron (oral GLP-1) expected H2 2026 — positive data would expand the total addressable market dramatically by removing injection barrier
picks.catalystWindow: H2 2026 (orforglipron Phase 3 data); ongoing manufacturing capacity additions
picks.entryNote: Valuation has de-rated meaningfully from 2024 all-time highs; forward P/E has compressed toward mid-30s from peak mid-50s — still premium but justified for a 20%+ EPS growth compounder with 5+ year runway; carry-over from May 4–7 positioning
picks.exitTrigger: Orforglipron Phase 3 failure on efficacy or safety, or a semaglutide/retatrutide competitor demonstrates durable superiority in head-to-head weight-loss trials
picks.keyRisk: U.S. drug price reform (IRA Part D negotiation expansion) compressing Mounjaro/Zepbound pricing earlier than consensus models assume

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: AWS re-accelerated to +28% growth in Q1 2026 (fastest in 15 quarters), with Trainium AI chip commitments topping $225B in backlog — signaling Amazon is building a durable custom silicon moat alongside its cloud infrastructure. Ackman added 65% to his AMZN position in Q4 2025, making it his second-largest holding at ~14% of Pershing Square. FCF yield remains robust at scale and FY EPS estimates have moved higher since Q1 reporting, providing fundamental underpinning for the +15% 6-month momentum.
picks.catalyst: AWS Q2 2026 growth rate disclosure (late July earnings) — acceleration above 30% would reprice cloud multiples across the sector; Trainium 3 availability announcement
picks.catalystWindow: Q2 earnings late July; ongoing AWS enterprise win announcements
picks.entryNote: Trading approximately 15% above 6-month-ago levels; AWS operating income is the primary valuation driver — at roughly 40x forward EPS the stock has upside to 50-55x if AWS growth sustains above 30%; sector rotation research highlights AMZN as 'cautious' on retail but structurally sound on cloud
picks.exitTrigger: AWS growth decelerates below 20% YoY for two consecutive quarters, or operating margin of AWS compresses below 35% due to custom silicon ramp costs
picks.keyRisk: Hyperscaler capex disappointment tail risk (Tail Risk #1 in risk briefing) — if GOOGL or MSFT signals capex pullback, AWS growth expectations reset lower

picks.name: RTX Corporation (Raytheon Technologies)
picks.rationale: RTX occupies the most direct intersection of the macro regime's two strongest tailwinds: the Iran War driving NATO/allied defense budget acceleration and commercial aerospace recovering to record backlogs. phaseData.macro:
Here is your full macro regime briefing as of **Friday, May 8, 2026**:

---

# 🏦 U.S. EQUITY MACRO REGIME BRIEFING — MAY 8, 2026

---

## 📊 THE DATA DASHBOARD

### Fed Policy

At its April 29 meeting, the FOMC left the federal funds target range at **3.50%–3.75%**.
 
The Fed acknowledged that Middle East developments have increased uncertainty around the economic outlook and said officials are watching risks to both inflation and employment.
 
The decision kept the Fed in a wait-and-see posture; nearly all voting members supported holding rates steady, while one favored a 0.25% rate cut, and three others disagreed with the statement's easing bias rather than the rate decision itself — showing a divide over how strongly the Fed should signal potential future cuts.
 
The Fed's median projection (March dot plot) still points to **one quarter-point cut in 2026**.
 
The March 2026 dot plot projected a 3.00%–3.25% Fed funds rate by year-end 2027.
 
Core PCE inflation stood at **3.0%** in February 2026, well above the 2% target.
 With the EFFR at 3.625% (midpoint) and the neutral rate (r*) widely estimated at 2.5–3.0%, the **real policy rate is mildly restrictive** — but the geopolitical energy shock is complicating the calculus.

---

### Yield Curve

Long-end yields drifted lower at month-end, with the **10-year easing to 4.39%** and the **30-year settling at 4.97%**.
 The 2-year is implied near ~3.90% given the prevailing spread data. 
The 10-year/2-year spread is **+0.50pp** and the 10-year/3-month spread is **+0.75pp**.
 
Breakeven inflation expectations ticked higher: the 5-year breakeven rose to **2.72%** and the 10-year breakeven climbed to **2.50%**, suggesting markets are pricing in a small upward bias on inflation expectations as the next FOMC meeting approaches.
 The curve is positively sloped but **flat** — not a recessionary signal, but term premium direction is being pushed higher by energy-driven inflation worries on the long end.

---

### Inflation

In March, CPI for all urban consumers rose **0.9% MoM** and **+3.3% YoY**, while the core CPI (all items less food and energy) increased **0.2% MoM** and **+2.6% YoY**.
 
Over the year through March 2026, the headline PCE price index was **3.5%**, 1.1 percentage points above the 2.4% pace over the year through March 2025, with fluctuations in energy prices a large contributor to the pickup.
 
CPI inflation for energy goods and services averaged **3.2% per month in Q1 2026**, and on a 12-month basis, energy price inflation was **+12.5%** through March 2026, contrasting with a 3.3% decline a year earlier.
 
The ISM Services Prices Paid index held at **70.7** in April 2026 — the highest two-month average since October 2022, with all 18 industries tracked by the ISM reporting higher input costs.
 The inflation trajectory is clearly **re-accelerating**, driven by the energy/geopolitical shock; core is stickier than the Fed would like but not yet runaway.

---

### Growth

As of **May 8**, the Atlanta Fed GDPNow model estimates real GDP growth (SAAR) for Q2 2026 at **+3.7%**, with the nowcast of real gross private domestic investment growth rising to 9.2%, partly offset by a slight decline in personal consumption expenditures growth to 2.6%.
 
The first BEA estimate of Q1 real GDP growth came in at **+2.0%**, 0.8pp above GDPNow's final Q1 nowcast.
 
April nonfarm payrolls came in at **+115K** (well above consensus of ~62K), with gains concentrated in health care (+37K), transportation and warehousing (+30K), and retail trade (+22K).
 
The ISM Manufacturing PMI held at **52.7** in April 2026, matching its highest level since August 2022.
 
The ISM Services PMI inched down to **53.6** in April from 54 in March, loosely aligned with expectations but remaining firmly above prior-year averages.
 
The services prices gauge held at 70.7, with companies citing higher fuel, diesel, copper, and freight costs due to the war, plus aluminum and lumber costs from tariffs.


---

### Credit & Liquidity

ICE BofA US HY OAS was **~277 bps** (2.77%) in May 2026.
 This is extraordinarily tight by historical standards (long-run average ~500 bps), signaling risk-on complacency — or genuine credit quality at cycle highs. 
The 2026 credit market outlook calls for a rangebound rate environment and a modest increase in spreads within corporate credit markets; markets expect the Fed to lower the funds rate toward 3%, keeping the 10-year Treasury yield between 3.75%–4.25%.
 
Worker wages continue to outpace inflation, despite elevated price levels associated with the Iranian conflict.
 The DXY is showing modest weakness reflecting the Fed's on-hold posture and safe-haven demand for energy-importing currencies.

---

### Earnings Backdrop

With 63% of S&P 500 companies having reported Q1 2026, **84%** have beaten EPS estimates — above the 5-year average of 78% and the 10-year average of 76%.
 
In aggregate, companies are reporting earnings **20.7% above estimates**, which would mark the highest surprise percentage since Q1 2021 (22.2%).
 
The blended net profit margin for Q1 2026 stood at **13.4%** — a new record since FactSet began tracking the metric in 2009, surpassing the prior record of 13.2% set in Q4 2025.
 
The forward 12-month P/E ratio is **20.9×**, above the 5-year average (19.9×) and above the 10-year average (18.9×).
 
For CY 2026, analysts are predicting year-over-year earnings growth of **+21.3%**.
 
The Q2 bottom-up EPS estimate increased 2.1% (to **$80.47 from $78.84**) from March 31 to April 30 — an unusual positive revision in the first month of a quarter.
 
The S&P 500 is trading at approximately **$7,327** as of May 8, 2026.


---

### Geopolitical / Event Tape

According to ISM's April survey commentary, sentiment remained mixed in the second month of the Iran War, with 69% of comments negative, and the war mentioned in 47% of responses alongside tariffs in 18%.
 
Geopolitical uncertainty remains an upside risk to the medium-term inflation outlook.
 
Kevin Warsh, the next Fed Chair nominee, previously supported reducing interest rates
 — creating a leadership transition wildcard for H2 2026 monetary policy.

---

## 📋 THE THREE PARAGRAPHS

---

### 1. 🔴 Regime Call: **Late-Cycle / Stagflationary Overhang**

This is a **late-cycle regime with an embedded stagflationary shock** — not yet contraction, but structurally more fragile than mid-2025. 
The FOMC left rates at 3.50%–3.75%
, sitting above consensus neutral (~2.75–3.00%), meaning real policy rates are mildly restrictive even as nominal growth remains elevated. 
Headline PCE has re-accelerated to 3.5% YoY
 while 
the labor market is gradually cooling but remaining broadly resilient.
 The key tension: 
the ISM Services Prices Paid index held at 70.7 in April
 — the classic late-cycle stagflationary fingerprint of cost-push inflation running ahead of output deceleration. The Fed is trapped: it cannot ease without stoking inflation, and 
federal government employment continued to decline
 while manufacturing employment is contracting, narrowing the Fed's ability to declare mission accomplished. We are in a **late-cycle expansion with an energy-shock overlay** — bullish earnings momentum has extended the runway, but the clock is ticking.

---

### 2. ✅ What's Working / ❌ What's Breaking

**Working:**
- 
**Information Technology, Communication Services, Financials, and Real Estate** are all reporting year-over-year revenue growth
, and 
Alphabet, Amazon, and Meta were the three largest contributors to index earnings growth, dominating Q1 upside.
 AI capex beneficiaries (semis, cloud infrastructure, power) remain the dominant alpha sources.
- 
Record blended net profit margins at 13.4%
 favor **quality compounders** — companies with pricing power and lean cost structures, particularly in Tech and Industrials.
- **Energy** sector: 
12-month energy price inflation at +12.5%
 is a direct tailwind for integrated oils, LNG exporters, and defense-adjacent energy infrastructure.
- 
HY OAS near 277 bps
 signals benign credit conditions — **IG credit** and **leveraged finance** markets remain open for business.

**Breaking:**
- 
Manufacturing employment is declining at the sharpest pace in four months (ISM Employment sub-index at 46.4)
, and 
services employment contracted for a second consecutive month.

- 
New orders in Services sank 7.1 points to 53.5 — the sharpest decrease in three years — as consumer order volumes were dented by the surge in prices since the start of the war.

- 
Valuations now embed an expectation that the earnings trajectory continues, and the market's selective reaction to Big Tech capital spending plans suggests investors are no longer giving open-ended credit for AI investment without evidence of return.

- **Consumer discretionary** and **small-cap / high-beta cyclicals**: with 
5-year breakeven inflation at 2.72%
 and the Fed on hold, rate-sensitive sectors face continued headwinds. Rate cuts look increasingly H2 2026 at best.

---

### 3. 👁️ What to Watch: The 3 Highest-Impact Catalysts

1. **April CPI (May 12, 8:30am ET) — #1 Priority.** 
The Consumer Price Index for April 2026 is scheduled to be released on May 12.
 The market must resolve whether the energy shock's 0.9% MoM March print was a one-off or is feeding into core. If April core CPI surprises above ~0.3% MoM, the one remaining Fed cut in the dot plot gets repriced away entirely, long-end yields spike, and the 20.9× forward P/E becomes untenable. A benign print (~0.2% core) reopens the easing narrative and would be a relief catalyst.

2. **Iran War / Middle East Escalation Trajectory — Ongoing.** 
The Iran War was mentioned in 47% of ISM Manufacturing respondent comments in April
, making it the single largest driver of input cost uncertainty. Any material ceasefire or de-escalation would likely cause a rapid reversal of the energy inflation shock, collapsing the stagflation narrative. Conversely, Strait of Hormuz disruption or expansion of the conflict would accelerate the re-pricing of the Fed's neutral rate higher and trigger spread widening from historically tight levels.

3. **Fed Chair Transition (Kevin Warsh Confirmation Process) — 1-3 Month Horizon.** 
Kevin Warsh, the next Fed Chair nominee, previously supported reducing interest rates
, but he is also known as a market structuralist with hawkish instincts on inflation credibility. His confirmation hearings will be parsed intensely for any divergence from Powell's current "wait-and-see" stance. A dovish signal could reprice front-end rates down 25-50bps and provide a meaningful multiple expansion catalyst; a hawkish lean — particularly on fiscal dominance risks — could reverse the April ATH rally.

---

> **Bottom Line for the Portfolio Manager:** This is a regime that rewards **quality, pricing power, and earnings visibility** — not leverage, duration, or speculation. The S&P 500's new all-time high near 7,382 is fundamentally justified by record margins and a 27%+ Q1 blended earnings growth rate, but the 20.9× forward P/E leaves no margin for error on either the inflation or the geopolitical front. Stay long the compounders; hedge the energy shock via integrated oil exposure; and resist chasing small-cap cyclicals until the ISM employment sub-indices re-enter expansion territory.

# 🏆 SECTOR ROTATION SCORECARD — MAY 8, 2026
### Long-Only, 1–5 Year Horizon | Ranked Top → Bottom

> **Macro Context:** 
As of early 2026, a major rotation is underway: after three years of mega-cap tech dominance, capital is aggressively flowing out of Silicon Valley and into the "Real Economy"—specifically industrial, materials, and consumer staples sectors—marking a transition from speculative digital growth to tangible physical assets.
 
The equal-weighted S&P 500 (RSP) has outperformed the cap-weighted S&P 500, one of the clearest indications of a broader rotation in sentiment and capital flows not seen in years.
 
The S&P 500 currently trades at approximately 22.2x forward P/E.


---

## TIER 1 — OVERWEIGHT / BUY THE DIP

---

### 🥇 1. INDUSTRIALS (Defense / Electrification / Machinery / Aerospace)
**Relative Strength Tier: LEADER**


Caterpillar (CAT) and GE Vernova (GEV) have surged as they provide the heavy machinery and power systems required for the "Copper and Kilowatts" phase of the AI data center build-out.
 
With infrastructure spending ramping up and supply chains normalizing, industrials like Caterpillar and GE Aerospace are outperforming, with XLI up ~11% YTD in 2026.


- **Valuation/Flow:** 
XLI had a total return of ~29.87% in the past year including dividends.
 Earnings revision breadth is net positive; defense names benefit from Iran war-related demand re-rating.
- **Business Cycle:** Early-to-mid expansion leader; fiscal stimulus tailwind is structural.
- **⚡ Why Now:** 
The "physicality" of AI is taking precedence over the "virtual" — 2026 is about the copper, cables, and kilowatts, with companies like Caterpillar seeing renewed orders from the domestic manufacturing boom sparked by new fiscal policies.


---

### 🥈 2. FINANCIALS (Banks / Capital Markets / Insurance / Fintech)
**Relative Strength Tier: LEADER**


Capital is flowing out of overextended mega-cap growth stocks and into undervalued industrials, financials, and mid-cap names.
 
The sector is rate-sensitive in both directions, but XLF is the right tilt when the yield curve is steepening and credit spreads are tight — it benefits from cyclical economic expansion and underperforms only when recession risk rises.


- **Valuation/Flow:** 
Energy, Materials, Health Care, and Financials all trade at a discount to the index.
 Banks and capital markets are direct beneficiaries of a steepening curve and active deal market.
- **Business Cycle:** Mid-cycle expansion leader; capital markets revenue (M&A, IPOs) typically surges.
- **⚡ Why Now:** 
Capital is rotating toward sectors with more visible cash flows and lower disruption risk, including professional services and finance.
 
XLF receives an outperform rating supported by robust bank profitability and resilient economic growth.


---

### 🥉 3. TECHNOLOGY / AI INFRASTRUCTURE / SEMIS (XLK / SOXX)
**Relative Strength Tier: COILING (recovering from Q1 drawdown)**


While the Nasdaq 100 retreated roughly 6% since the start of 2026 following the "DeepSeek AI shock," the equal-weight index touched record highs, suggesting the rotation has been from software SaaS to physical AI infrastructure rather than an exit from tech entirely.


- **Valuation/Flow:** 
Tech has rebounded strongly in 2026, with AI, cloud computing, and cybersecurity driving earnings growth — Nvidia and Microsoft both posted double-digit revenue increases.
 
Hyperscaler capex is expected to reach a record $485 billion in 2026, potentially exceeding $515 billion.

- **Business Cycle:** Structural secular grower; currently in a reset after froth burned off.
- **⚡ Why Now:** 
Technology and Communications are seeing significant discounts to expected growth rates
 — semis and AI infrastructure hardware are the "picks and shovels" of an irreversible capex supercycle, making the current dip a 1–5 year entry point.

---

### 4. ENERGY — UPSTREAM + MIDSTREAM (XLE / AMLP)
**Relative Strength Tier: LEADER (may be maturing)**

 At these prices, even a modest deceleration in government contract wins or U.S. commercial growth disappointing vs. the ~115% guide would reprice the stock 30–50%. Notably, any broader risk-off regime would punish the highest-multiple names first, and PLTR sits at the apex.

### 🔴 3. SNOW — Snowflake, Inc.
**Risk Score: HIGH**

A one-two punch of deteriorating margin guidance and active litigation overhang. 
SNOW has already lost about 35.7% year-to-date in 2026, and a shareholder class action lawsuit filed in late April alleged the company misrepresented its consumption revenue growth.
 
The company reported a net loss of $1.33B for the full fiscal year, and investors began questioning whether rapid growth alone could justify the stock's historically high valuation.

The company lowered its non-GAAP operating margin outlook to the 8–9% range, down from previous internal targets of 10%+.
 
The shift toward AI workloads is more compute-intensive and carries lower gross margins than traditional SQL data warehousing.


**Competitive squeeze:** 
SNOW faces intensifying competition from Databricks and hyperscalers, but the enterprise data and AI market remains large enough to support multiple winners.
 The risk: the hyperscalers (AWS, Azure, GCP) can subsidize their own data platforms to win enterprise workloads at SNOW's expense.

Litigation timelines are unpredictable; the case could take several years to resolve, and legal overhang will likely limit the multiple expansion the stock could otherwise achieve.
